one_line_takeaway: A modality-organized (image/video/audio/speech) taxonomy survey of MLLM adversarial attacks and defenses that expands past image-text-only surveys and highlights how attack research far outpaces defenses, especially in audio and speech.
key_finding: Across modalities the attack literature is far more developed than the defense literature, and reported attacks are highly effective while assuming diverse access levels. As reported by the cited primary works: backdoor attacks are especially potent (AnyDoor 98.5% ASR, VL-Trojan >99% ASR at 0.5% poisoning, cross-domain triggers >97% ASR under domain shift), data poisoning succeeds with tiny budgets (Shadowcast >80% ASR with <50 poisoned examples), and audio/speech modalities are underexplored yet vulnerable (indirect audio injection >90% ASR on LLaVA). Defenses remain modality-specific and lack holistic cross-modal metrics, and the field lacks standardized evaluation protocols.
core_contribution: A survey (not an experimental paper) that organizes existing work on the adversarial robustness of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) into a modality-based taxonomy spanning image, video, audio, and speech, covering BOTH attacks and defenses. Its self-claimed novelty over prior surveys (Table 1) is scope expansion: earlier surveys were restricted to text or image-text (LVLM) settings, whereas this one adds video, audio, and speech modalities and their defenses. It also contributes (i) a catalog of representative datasets and evaluation benchmarks per modality (Table 2), and (ii) an observation that the literature is imbalanced, with far more attack studies than defense studies across modalities. The taxonomy (Fig. 1) is the central artifact.
threat_model: Union of threats catalogued from the reviewed primary works, not a single novel threat model. Attacker goals: force incorrect/harmful outputs, bypass safety alignment, or corrupt reliability. Vectors organized by modality and access level: (1) Adversarial attacks under full access (white-box: gradient/diffusion-based visual perturbations, image hijacking, verbose-image energy-latency attacks, Stop-Reasoning against Chain-of-Thought) and restricted access (black/gray-box transfer via CLIP/BLIP surrogates, query-based, safety-eval frameworks like Unicorn/B-AVIBench). (2) Jailbreak attacks: cross-modal inconsistency, prompt manipulation (typographic images, universal master key), and training-phase jailbreaks. (3) Data-integrity attacks: data poisoning and backdoors, active at both training and inference. Extends to video (temporal-coherence, autonomous-driving scenario attacks), audio (embodied/robot control, indirect instruction injection), and speech (spoofing, voice jailbreaks). Emphasizes cross-modal manipulation as a distinct expanded attack surface versus unimodal models.
limitations: As a survey it introduces no new method, dataset, or measurement; all effectiveness figures are as-reported by the cited works and were not independently reproduced or normalized, so cross-study numbers are not directly comparable. Coverage is modest (~23 references cited) relative to the fast-moving field, and video/audio/speech sections are thin (a handful of works each), reflecting rather than resolving the literature imbalance the authors flag. The paper offers no systematic-review protocol (no search strategy or inclusion criteria) and no quantitative meta-analysis; defense coverage is limited to a few representative systems per modality.

datasets_used: MS-COCO (image; catalogued in Table 2, not run by authors)
quantitative_results: AnyDoor test-time backdoor [Lu et al. 2024]: 98.5% attack success rate across multiple MLLMs, robust under noise and cropping (as reported by cited work)
VL-Trojan [Liang et al. 2024a]: >99% attack success rate while poisoning only 0.5% of instruction-tuning examples (as reported)
Cross-domain backdoor [Xu et al. 2024b]: >97% attack success rate sustained under domain shift (as reported)
Shadowcast clean-label poisoning [Liang et al. 2024b]: >80% attack success rate with fewer than 50 poisoned examples (as reported)
Indirect audio instruction injection [Bagdasaryan et al. 2023]: >90% attack success rate on LLaVA (as reported)
baselines_compared: Prior surveys compared for positioning only (Table 1), not experimental baselines: Chowdhury et al. 2024 (text attacks, LLMs); Fan et al. 2024 (image, attack-only); Liu et al. 2024a (image-text attacks); Liu et al. 2024b (image-text safety, attack+defense). This survey positions itself as the only one covering multimodal (image/video/audio/speech) attack AND defense.
